title: The Babes in the Woods
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Something characteristic,ן¿½ eh?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Humph! I reckon you mean by that
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Something that happened in our way,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Here at the crossinן¿½ of Big Pine Flat.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Times arenן¿½t now as they used to be,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When gold was flush and the boys were frisky,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And a man would pull out his battery
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For anything maybe the price of whiskey.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nothing of that sort, eh? Thatן¿½s strange!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Why, I thought you might be diverted
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Hearing how Jones of Red Rock Range
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Drawed his ן¿½hint to the unconverted,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And saying, ן¿½Whar will you have it?ן¿½ shot
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Cherokee Bob at the last debating!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What was the question I forgot,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But Jones didnן¿½t like Bobן¿½s way of stating.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Nothing of that kind, eh? You mean
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Something milder? Letן¿½s see! O Joe!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Tell to the stranger that little scene
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Out of the ן¿½Babes in the Woods.ן¿½ You know,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Babesן¿½ was the name that we gave ן¿½em, sir,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Two lean lads in their teens, and greener
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Than even the belt of spruce and fir
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where they built their nest, and each day grew leaner.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No one knew where they came from. None
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Cared to ask if they had a mother.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Runaway schoolboys, maybe. One
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Tall and dark as a spruce; the other
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Blue and gold in the eyes and hair,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Soft and low in his speech, but rarely
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Talking with us; and we didnן¿½t care
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To get at their secret at all unfairly.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For they were so quiet, so sad and shy,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Content to trust each other solely,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That somehow weן¿½d always shut one eye,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And never seem to observe them wholly
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As they passed to their work. ן¿½Twas a worn-out claim,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And it paid them grub. They could live without it,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For the boys had a way of leaving game
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In their tent, and forgetting all about it.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet no one asked for their secret. Dumb
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It lay in their big eyesן¿½ heavy hollows.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It was understood that no one should come
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To their tent unawares, save the bees and swallows.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So they lived alone. Until one warm night
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I was sitting here at the tent-door, so, sir!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When out of the sunsetן¿½s rosy light
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Up rose the Sheriff of Mariposa.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I knew at once there was something wrong,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For his hand and his voice shook just a little,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And there isnן¿½t much you can fetch along
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To make the sinews of Jack Hill brittle.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Go warn the Babes!ן¿½ he whispered, hoarse;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Tell them Iן¿½m coming to get and scurry;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For Iן¿½ve got a story thatן¿½s bad, and worse,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½ve got a warrant: G-d d n it, hurry!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Too late! they had seen him cross the hill;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I ran to their tent and found them lying
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dead in each otherן¿½s arms, and still
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Clasping the drug they had taken flying.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And there lay their secret cold and bare,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Their life, their trial the old, old story!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For the sweet blue eyes and the golden hair
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was a womanן¿½s shame and a womanן¿½s glory.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Who were they?ן¿½ Ask no more, or ask
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The sun that visits their grave so lightly;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ask of the whispering reeds, or task
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The mourning crickets that chirrup nightly.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ All of their life but its love forgot,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Everything tender and soft and mystic,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ These are our Babes in the Woods, youן¿½ve got,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Well human nature thatן¿½s characteristic.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Bret Harte (Francis) represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Bret Harte (Francis)'s bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
